# NJ Housing Affordability and Labor Market Analysis

*Generated by RAIL research agent (gemini-flash-latest) at 2026-05-19T15:16:55Z*
*Data source: FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data)*
*Series: NJSTHPI, NJURN, CPIAUCSL | Period: 2015-01-01 – 2025-10-01*

---

## Facts

Between January 2015 and October 2025, the New Jersey Housing Price Index (NJSTHPI) exhibited a remarkable upward trajectory, rising from a baseline of 472.09 to a terminal value of 939.31. This represents a total nominal increase of 99.0% over 44 quarterly observations. The trend moved through three distinct phases: a period of steady, moderate appreciation from 2015 through 2019, a brief period of market hesitation during the initial 2020 COVID-19 shock, and an aggressive post-COVID surge. This final phase saw the index accelerate rapidly, nearly doubling the 2015 valuation by the end of the data series in late 2025.

The relationship between the labor market and housing prices during this period was notably decoupled. While the New Jersey unemployment rate (NJURN) experienced a massive spike to approximately 15% in April 2020, this historic level of joblessness failed to suppress housing prices. Instead of the price correction typically associated with high unemployment, the NJSTHPI continued to climb throughout the pandemic. By the end of the reporting period in March 2026, the unemployment rate had settled at 4.7%, a 31.9% decrease from the 6.9% recorded in January 2015, suggesting that the housing market remained insulated from labor volatility by low inventory and high demand.

The divergence between housing costs and general inflation highlights a significant decline in real affordability. While the Consumer Price Index (CPIAUCSL) rose by 41.6%—moving from 234.75 in 2015 to 332.41 by April 2026—housing prices grew at more than double that pace. After adjusting for this inflation, the real housing price change stands at 57.4%. This indicates that the cost of homeownership in New Jersey has far outstripped the growth in the cost of other goods and services, placing a substantial financial burden on new market entrants compared to the 2015 baseline.

Key inflection points in the data define this decade of economic shifts. The series began on January 1, 2015, with an HPI of 472.09 and an unemployment rate of 6.9%. The most significant labor market disruption occurred in April 2020, when unemployment hit its 15% peak. Despite this, the housing index continued its climb to reach 939.31 by October 1, 2025. By the final data points in early 2026, the economy reached a new equilibrium characterized by a 4.7% unemployment rate and a CPI of 332.41, marking a period where real estate became the dominant driver of wealth and cost-of-living increases in the region.

## Interpretation

Real (inflation-adjusted) housing prices in NJ moved by +57.4% over the window;
unemployment trended from 6.9% to 4.7%. See claims block for grounded statements.

## Open Questions

- How did NJ housing growth compare to neighboring states (NY, PA, CT)?
- What is the contribution of mortgage rates to the affordability change?
